---
title: "Front Matter"
---

::: {.archive-notice}
**Source:** Pages 1--16 of *MintonThesis.pdf* (July 2009). Text extracted from PDF.
:::

# Work and its Other: A Multiple-perspective Analysis of Incapacity Benefit Reform

**Jonathan William Minton**\
Department of Sociology\
University of York\
July 2009

## Abstract

This is a thesis of two halves: five 'theory' chapters and five 'empirical' chapters, prefaced by an introduction, and then followed by a conclusion.

The five 'empirical' chapters are based around understanding the development and roll-out of a programme of Incapacity Benefits reform known as 'Pathways to Work', which was first introduced in 2002. Pathways to Work has since been implemented in all Jobcentre Plus offices across the UK, and so this half of the thesis could be seen as a case study of a 'successfully implemented' government policy.

The five 'theoretical' chapters introduce, explain, and interpret a range of theories about social processes that operate at various temporal and spatial scales. These theories range from the very small scale (human neurobiology) to the very large scale (archaeology and biogeography), and conclude with mid-scale theories relating to observed and intuitive everyday interpersonal interactions.

The two halves of the thesis are intended to be complementary, with the 'theoretical' half illuminating the 'empirical' half and the 'empirical' half illuminating the 'theoretical' half. The thesis concludes by offering a number of policy suggestions, and making the complementarity between thesis halves more explicit.

## List of Contents

- List of Contents
- List of Figures
- List of Tables
- Acknowledgements
- Author's Declaration

1. Introduction
    - 1.1 Theory Section
    - 1.2 Evidence Section
    - 1.3 Summary and Conclusion
2. Organisms, Decisions and Emotions: Some Theories of Very Small Scale Social Phenomena
    - 2.1 Introduction
    - 2.2 Health Inequalities and Human Physiology
    - 2.3 The Individual and the Organism
    - 2.4 Organisms and Boundaries
    - 2.5 Organisms as Abstract Machines
    - 2.6 Objects and Emotions
    - 2.7 Feelings and Self
    - 2.8 Centralisation and the Proto-self
    - 2.9 Emotions and Decision-making
    - 2.10 The As-if-body-loop
    - 2.11 From Memories and Dispositions to Habits and Habitus
    - 2.12 Conclusion: Sketching an Abstract Machine
3. Empires, Elites and States: Some Theories of Very Large Scale Social Phenomena
    - 3.1 Introduction
    - 3.2 Evolution and Social Processes
    - 3.3 Food, Social Structure, and Societal Complexity
    - 3.4 Stages of Societal Complexity
    - 3.5 Societal Complexity and Environmental Carrying Capacity
    - 3.6 Types of Social Problems
    - 3.7 Kleptocracy and the Growth of Inequality
4. Obedience and Hierarchy: Some Theories of Small Scale Social Phenomena
    - 4.1 Introduction
    - 4.2 Description
    - 4.3 The Break-point
    - 4.4 Predictions
    - 4.5 Modes of Response
    - 4.6 Variations
    - 4.7 Summary & Discussion
5. Bureaucracies and Organisations: Some Theories of Large Scale Social Phenomena
    - 5.1 Introduction
    - 5.2 Weber's Anatomy of Bureaucracy
    - 5.3 Durable Multi-level Hierarchies
    - 5.4 Modernity: the transition from traditional to legal rule
    - 5.5 Functionaries and Roles
    - 5.6 Writing, Law and Literacy
    - 5.7 The Self-sustaining Qualities of Bureaucratic Structures
6. Some theories of observable social phenomena and their implications as socioeconomic mechanisms
    - 6.1 Introduction
    - 6.2 Relational Models Theory
    - 6.3 Mode 1: Communal Sharing (CS)
    - 6.4 Mode 2: Authority Ranking, and Mode 3: Equality Matching
    - 6.5 Mode 4: Market Pricing
    - 6.6 Substantivism
    - 6.7 Conclusion: Bureaucracies, Metrics, and Modes
7. The Emergence of the Work-As-Employment and State-As-Providers Norms
    - 7.1 Introduction
    - 7.2 A Cross-sectional Slice through Contemporary Economic Categorisation
    - 7.3 Longitudinal Considerations: The Historical Origins of Recorded Unemployment
    - 7.4 Two Concepts: 'Epistemic Poverty' and 'Gaming the System'
    - 7.5 Concluding Remarks
8. Invalidity Benefit and Incapacity Benefit
    - 8.1 Introduction
    - 8.2 On the Conditions Under Which the Qualities of Government Benefit (And Related) Statistics Arose
    - 8.3 Longitudinal Investigations
    - 8.4 On the relationship between sickness, employment, and social class amongst males
    - 8.5 Cross-Sectional Trends and Characteristics
    - 8.6 Summary and Discussion
9. Pathways to Work: Qualitative Descriptions
    - 9.1 Introduction
    - 9.2 What is Pathways to Work?
    - 9.3 Pathways to Work: Plans and Pilot Schemes
    - 9.4 Key Elements of Pathways to Work
    - 9.5 Incapacity Benefit Personal Advisers (IBPAs)
    - 9.6 Work-Focussed Interviews (WFIs)
    - 9.7 The Screening Tool
    - 9.8 The Condition Management Programme (CMP)
    - 9.9 Section Summary and Discussion
10. Pathways to Work: Secondary Analysis of Early Quantitative Evidence of Programme Effectiveness
    - 10.1 Introduction: 'The Graph'
    - 10.2 Inference
    - 10.3 Inferring Treatment Effects from The Graph
    - 10.4 Off-flow Rates and Number Needed to Treat
    - 10.5 Cost Per Successful Intervention: an illustrative approximation
    - 10.6 Aggregate Effects
    - 10.7 Introducing the Number Needed to Observe (NNO)
    - 10.8 Subsection Concluding Remarks
11. Pathways to Work: Secondary Analysis of Later Quantitative Evidence of Programme Effectiveness
    - 11.1 Introduction
    - 11.2 The Policy Studies Report: Methodology
    - 11.3 Reported Impact Estimates
    - 11.4 The Motivational Explanation and Employment Outcomes
    - 11.5 Concluding Remarks
12. Discussions and Conclusion
    - 12.1 Introduction
    - 12.2 The Five-year Discussion: Evidence-based politics and the rhetorical use of statistics
    - 12.3 The Thirty-year Discussion: On the nonlinear relationship between 'job fitness' (including physical fitness) and employability
    - 12.4 The Hundred-year Discussion
    - 12.5 Policy Recommendations
    - 12.6 Summary and Discussion

- Appendix: The Screening Tool
- Glossary
- Bibliography

## List of Figures

- Figure 1.1 Relationship between thesis sections and chapters
- Figure 1.2 Relationship between theory chapters
- Figure 1.3 Relationship between evidence chapters
- Figure 2.1 Levels of Life Regulation
- Figure 3.1 Two 'modes of living'
- Figure 3.2 Mergers and Acquisitions
- Figure 4.1 Milgram's Obedience to Authority experiments: Predicted break-points
- Figure 4.2 Actual, predicted, and chosen shock levels
- Figure 5.1 Graphical representation of Charles Tilly's 'codes'
- Figure 7.1 International Labour Organisation classifications
- Figure 8.1 'Reasons for Inactivity'
- Figure 8.2 Number of people on benefits, 1979--2005
- Figure 8.3 IVB/IB and ILO Unemployment levels within UK, 1975--2007
- Figure 8.4 Working age population and IVB/IB claimant numbers, 1975--2007
- Figure 8.5 IVB/IB claimants per 1000 people of working age, 1975--2007
- Figure 8.6 Numbers of male IVB/IB claimants and female working age economic activity rates
- Figure 8.7 Female IVB/IB claimants per 1000 male IVB/IB claimants, 1975--2007
- Figure 8.8 Index of IVB/IB population to overall working population (1975=100)
- Figure 8.9 'Inactivity rates for men with high levels of qualifications by age group and birth cohort; 1986 to 2001'
- Figure 8.10 'Inactivity rates for men with no qualifications by age group and birth cohort; 1986 to 2001'
- Figure 8.11 Proportions employed according to socioeconomic group, 1975--2003
- Figure 8.12 Proportion employed according to limiting longstanding illness and socioeconomic group
- Figure 8.13 Proportions unemployed according to limiting longstanding illness and socioeconomic group
- Figure 8.14 Proportions inactive according to limiting longstanding illness and socioeconomic group
- Figure 8.15 Suggested relationship between economic activity, personal health and economic 'health'
- Figure 8.16 Reasons for economic inactivity amongst males by age group
- Figure 8.17 Reasons for economic inactivity amongst females by age group
- Figure 8.18 Numbers of IB claimants by gender and age group, May 2007
- Figure 8.19 Over- and under-representation of IB claimants at different age groups
- Figure 9.1 Theory Underlying Screening Tool
- Figure 9.2 RTWCs as a proportion of initial WFIs
- Figure 9.3 RTWCs as a proportion of recorded job entries
- Figure 10.1--10.15 *(see Chapter 10)*
- Figure 11.1--11.7 *(see Chapter 11)*
- Figure 12.1--12.14 *(see Chapter 12)*

## List of Tables

- Table 3.1 Jared Diamond's 'Types of Societies'
- Table 4.1 Qualitative interpretation of results to different types of Milgram experiment
- Table 5.1 Charles Tilly's 4 types of reasons
- Table 6.1 Relationship between terms used by Polanyi & Fiske
- Table 8.1 Pairwise correlation coefficients, employment, inactivity, and unemployment, given social class and health, 1973--2003
- Table 9.1 Key Incapacity Benefit events, according to the DWP
- Table 9.2 Pathways evaluations as of 19 March 2008
- Table 9.3 'Gains to be realised by those moving into work from an incapacity benefit whilst the return-to-work credit is payable'
- Table 10.1 Six month off-flow rate estimates, control & treatment regions
- Table 10.2 Six month off-flow Number Needed to Treat estimates
- Table 10.3 Pathways NNT estimates for employment outcome estimates (54%/57% conversion)
- Table 10.4 Pathways NNT estimates for employment outcome estimates (52% conversion)
- Table 11.1 'Effect of Pathways on receipt of incapacity benefits -- summary measures for April 2004 areas'
- Table A.1 The Screening Tool

## Acknowledgements

Progress towards completing this thesis has been approximately sigmoid in shape: slow in the first year, increasingly fast in the second year ("I think you're about six months from completion..."), and then slow again in the third year ("...maybe not."). So slow, in fact, that the third year stretched over into a fourth year as, well, 'other stuff happened'. Sigmoid curves are asymptotic, never quite reaching their destination. I want to apologise to my thesis advisory 'team' -- Roger Burrows, Danny Dorling, and Kate Pickett -- for at times making the sigmoid curve look more like prophecy than approximation. I also want to thank Roger and Danny for allowing me the opportunity to begin the PhD in the first place, and for then allowing it to develop (mutate?) from a GIS project to something closer to a work of applied political philosophy.

I would also like to thank Stuart Adam, Jenny Allen, Kelly Benneworth, Beverly Geesin, Roger Bowles, Jonathan Bradshaw, Gill Callaghan, Colin Campbell, Stephen Clayton, Thomas Cochrane, Jacqueline Davidson, Richard Dorsett, Paul Drew, Jean-Baptiste Gouyon, Steve Hall, Martin Ham, Nicola Holt, Gary King, Richard King, Graham Lewis, Daryl Martin, Duncan McVicar, Rob Mellows, Dan Mercea, Andrew Oswald, Theo Papadopoulos, Simon Parker, Nicholas Pleace, Darren Reed, Barbara Robertson, Roy Sainsbury, Simon Shaw, Phil Stanworth, Charles Tilly, Merran Toerien, Emma Uprichard, Roberto de Vogli, Simon Walker, Gemma Warren, Richard Wilkinson, Astrid Winkler, and Simon Winlow. Each of these people, I think, has contributed towards my thinking about the concepts, facts, and ideas developed over the course of the PhD. They, of course, deserve some of the credit but none of the blame for what is presented here, which they may disagree with entirely.

Finally, I would like to thank my mother, Helen Minton, for eagle-eyed proofreading, and to her and my father, Nigel Minton, for allowing me to soliloquise at length to them about many of the ideas contained within this thesis, even where such soliloquies were unsolicited; and to thank Natalie Wheatley, also for proof-reading, and for offering persistent encouragement that I "complete and submit it", despite not really knowing what 'it' is.

## Author's Declaration

I hereby declare that the material in this thesis is solely my own and has not previously been published.
